yaml.title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
yaml.description: Study note on Chapter 3 of Funnell and Rogers' Purposeful Program Theory.

markdown.markdown:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 64-81 Chapter 3
This note summarises Chapter 3, "Common Myths and Traps," from Funnell and Rogers' *Purposeful Program Theory* [@funnell_rogers_2011_purposeful_program_theory]. It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter. ## Core Argument The value of program theory depends on avoiding common myths and practical traps, especially the belief that drawing a diagram is enough or that one standard model can fit every evaluation purpose. ## Study Summary The chapter clears away myths that weaken practice: program theory is not brand new, there is not one correct way to do it, it can be a serious evaluation model, and it does not necessarily require excessive time. The authors also challenge the idea that a theory is untestable or that causality can simply be assumed once a model is drawn. The traps are more operational. Program theory can become a compliance artifact, a vague outcomes list, an overcomplicated wall chart, or a model that ignores unintended outcomes and contextual variation. A particularly important trap is developing a theory for an evaluation and then failing to use it in evaluation design, data collection, analysis, and reporting. The chapter’s stance is pragmatic: program theory should be purposeful, proportionate, explicit, and used. A modest but well-targeted theory is better than an elaborate representation that nobody uses to make decisions. ## Key Concepts - Myth: a mistaken belief about what program theory is or requires. - Trap: a recurring practice problem that undermines the usefulness of a program theory. - Purposeful proportionality: investing enough effort for the intended use without turning the exercise into an end in itself. - Unintended results: outcomes that are outside the intended chain but still matter for evaluation. ## Chapter Structure The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics: - Some Common Myths - Myth 1: A New Approach - Myth 2: One Way to Do It - Myth 3: Not a Good Evaluation Model - Myth 4: Too Much Time - Myth 5: Just Draw It - Myth 6: Can’t Really Test It - Myth 7: Assume Causality - Traps To Avoid When Developing And Using Program Theory - Strategies to Avoid This Trap - Trap 2: Having a Poor Theory of Change - Trap 3: Poorly Specifying Intended Results - Trap 4: Ignoring Unintended Results - Trap 5: Oversimplifying ## Practical Implications - Choose the level of detail to match the decision or evaluation use. - Build in explicit attention to unintended outcomes and alternative explanations. - Make sure the final theory controls actual choices about measures, methods, and reporting. ## Connections - Previous: [2. Variations of Program Theory over Time](chapter-02-variations-of-program-theory-over-time.qmd) - Next: [4. Scoping Intended Uses](chapter-04-scoping-intended-uses.qmd) - Related synthesis: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd) - Practice synthesis: [Evaluation Practice Implications](evaluation-practice-implications.qmd) ## Study Prompts 1. What is the chapter's main claim about common myths and traps? 2. Which assumptions would need evidence before using this idea in an evaluation design? 3. How would the chapter change the way an evaluator scopes indicators, methods, or reporting? 4. Where could the guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template? ## References
